_comment: Hand-authored System Design deck. Merged into trainer/catalog.json by scripts/build_catalog.py. Framework adapted from Hello Interview's delivery framework (https://www.hellointerview.com/learn/system-design/in-a-hurry/delivery). type=framework drills the R-C-A-H-D sequence; type=question applies it to a canonical problem.

cards.title: The R-C-A-H-D framework
cards.front: Recall the 5 steps of the delivery framework and their time budgets.

recall: R — Requirements (~5m): functional + non-functional + capacity estimate
C — Core Entities (~2m): the nouns your API & DB use
A — API / Interface (~5m): one endpoint per functional requirement
(Data Flow ~5m: optional, only for data-pipeline questions)
H — High-Level Design (~10-15m): boxes & arrows for the functional reqs
D — Deep Dives (~10m): defend the non-functional reqs & bottlenecks

cards.title: Step R — Requirements
cards.front: What three things do you produce in the Requirements step, and how long?

recall: Functional: 'Users should be able to...' — the 2-3 CORE features only, park the rest as out-of-scope
Non-functional (the -ilities): scale, latency, consistency vs availability (CAP), durability, read:write ratio
Capacity estimate: only numbers that CHANGE a decision (e.g. 5 TB/day -> partition; 1M RPS -> CDN+cache)
This list is the contract your Deep Dives will defend later

cards.title: Step C — Core Entities
cards.front: What is the Core Entities step for, and how long?

recall: Name the handful of nouns your system centers on (User, Tweet, Ride, File...)
They become your data model and the payloads your API returns
A bulleted list is enough — do NOT design the schema yet
Gives you shared vocabulary with the interviewer before you build

cards.title: Step A — API / Interface
cards.front: How do you approach the API step?

recall: One endpoint per functional requirement — no more
REST for CRUD-ish systems, RPC for action-y ones
Note pagination (cursor), streaming, or websockets where relevant
Show a reasonable interface, then MOVE ON — don't gold-plate

cards.title: Step H — High-Level Design
cards.front: What is the goal of the High-Level Design step?

recall: Minimum components that make EVERY functional requirement work end-to-end
Typical spine: client -> gateway/LB -> service -> DB / cache / queue / blob store
Trace one request all the way through the boxes
Breadth first — resist deep-diving here

cards.title: Step D — Deep Dives
cards.front: What do Deep Dives cover, and why do they matter?

recall: Go back to the NON-functional list and defend each item
Scaling a hot component, caching, partitioning/sharding, a queue for spikes
Consistency handling, a specific algorithm choice, failure/edge cases
This is where senior / staff signal is earned

cards.title: Non-functional requirements checklist
cards.front: Which non-functional dimensions do you scan in every question?

recall: Scale — reads/writes per second, data volume, fan-out
Latency — what must feel instant (reads) vs what can lag (writes/analytics)
Consistency vs Availability — where is stale data OK vs not (CAP)
Durability — can we ever lose this data?
Read:write ratio — usually read-heavy; decides where caches/replicas go
Pick the 3-4 that actually bite THIS system; don't list all of them

cards.title: Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for a URL shortener.

functional: Create a short URL from a long URL (optional custom alias & expiry)
Redirect a short URL to the original long URL
nonfunctional: Read-heavy (100:1) — redirects must be low-latency
High availability for redirects; short codes must be unique
Scale: ~1B URLs, redirects at high RPS
hld: Client -> API service -> DB (key-value: shortCode -> longUrl)
Redirect path: read shortCode, fetch longUrl, return 302
Cache hot short codes (Redis) in front of the DB
deepDives: Unique code generation: counter + base62 (short, sequential) vs hash-of-URL (dedupes, needs collision check). Counter via a distributed range service / Redis INCR
Scale reads: CDN + Redis cache; DB read replicas — most redirects never hit primary
Partition the KV store by shortCode; short codes are ~7 base62 chars for ~3.5T combos

cards.title: Design Twitter / a News Feed
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for a social news feed.

functional: Post a tweet
Follow users
View a home timeline of tweets from people you follow
nonfunctional: Read-heavy; timeline load must be fast (<200ms)
Eventual consistency OK (a tweet can appear a few seconds late)
Handle celebrity fan-out (millions of followers)
hld: Write path: Tweet service -> Tweet DB + fan-out service
Fan-out-on-write: push tweetId into each follower's precomputed timeline cache (Redis list)
Read path: GET /feed reads the precomputed list — fast
deepDives: Celebrity problem: hybrid fan-out — push for normal users, but pull (merge at read time) for users who follow celebrities, to avoid millions of writes per tweet
Feed ranking: store tweetIds, hydrate tweet content from cache at read time
Storage: shard tweets by userId; timeline caches in Redis with a bounded length

cards.title: Design a Chat App (WhatsApp / Messenger)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for real-time 1:1 and group chat.

functional: Send/receive messages in real time (1:1 and group)
Delivery & read receipts
Deliver messages sent while a user is offline
nonfunctional: Low latency for delivery; ordered per conversation
High availability; messages must not be lost (durable)
Huge concurrent connection count
api: WebSocket: send(convId, text); server pushes incoming messages
hld: Clients hold persistent WebSocket to a Chat/Gateway server
A registry (Redis) maps userId -> which server holds their connection
Message service persists then routes to the recipient's server; offline -> stored for pull on reconnect
deepDives: Connection routing at scale: pub/sub (Redis/Kafka) between gateway servers so any server can reach any online user
Ordering & delivery: per-conversation sequence numbers; ACKs drive sent/delivered/read state
Offline delivery: durable message store + per-user 'last read' offset; sync on reconnect. Group fan-out writes to each member's inbox

cards.title: Design Uber (ride-sharing)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for matching riders to nearby drivers.

functional: Riders request a ride from A to B
Match rider to a nearby available driver
Track driver location live during the trip
nonfunctional: Low latency matching; frequent location updates (write-heavy)
High availability; strong consistency on a match (one driver, one rider)
Geospatial queries at city scale
api: WebSocket for match + live trip updates
hld: Location service ingests high-frequency driver pings into an in-memory geo index
Ride service queries nearby drivers, offers the ride, confirms a match
Notification via websocket to matched driver & rider
deepDives: Geospatial index: geohash or QuadTree / H3 cells; query the rider's cell + neighbors for candidate drivers
Location writes: don't persist every ping to the primary DB — keep current location in Redis, only snapshot periodically
Match consistency: lock/atomic claim so a driver isn't double-booked; timeout & re-offer if a driver doesn't accept

cards.title: Design Ticketmaster
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for booking event seats.

functional: Browse events and available seats
Reserve/hold a seat, then purchase it
nonfunctional: Strong consistency — never sell the same seat twice
Handle massive spikes (popular on-sales / thundering herd)
Reads (browsing) far outnumber writes (booking)
hld: Booking service with a relational DB (ACID) as source of truth for seat state
Search/browse served from a read-optimized store + cache
Seat hold with a TTL, then confirm on payment
deepDives: No double-booking: reserve a seat with a distributed lock or a DB row lock / status transition (AVAILABLE->HELD->BOOKED) inside a transaction
Holds expire: TTL (Redis key or a reservation row + expiry sweeper) auto-releases unpaid seats
Thundering herd on hot events: a virtual waiting queue admits users at a controlled rate; cache the seat map and update via events

cards.title: Design Dropbox / Google Drive
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for file storage & sync.

functional: Upload / download files
Sync files across a user's devices
Share files with other users
nonfunctional: Durable — never lose a file; highly available
Large files (GBs); efficient sync of small changes
Read-heavy (downloads)
hld: Metadata service (DB) separate from blob storage (S3) for the bytes
Client uploads chunks directly to blob store via presigned URLs
Sync: clients poll/subscribe to a changes feed keyed on metadata versions
deepDives: Chunking: split files into fixed chunks, hash each; only upload/download changed chunks (dedup + fast sync)
Upload path: presigned S3 URLs so bytes bypass app servers; metadata committed after chunks land
Sync & conflicts: per-file version vectors; CDN for downloads; large files use multipart upload

cards.title: Design YouTube / Netflix (video streaming)
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for video upload & streaming.

functional: Upload a video
Stream/watch a video (adaptive quality)
nonfunctional: Very read-heavy; smooth playback (low buffering) globally
Durable storage of originals; huge bandwidth
Upload is async / eventually available
hld: Upload -> raw blob store -> async transcoding pipeline (multiple resolutions/bitrates)
Transcoded segments stored in blob store, fronted by a CDN
Player fetches an adaptive-bitrate manifest (HLS/DASH), pulls segments from nearest CDN edge
deepDives: Transcoding: fan-out per (resolution x codec) as parallel jobs via a queue; segment the video so segments transcode independently
Delivery: CDN is the whole game — cache segments at the edge; ABR lets the client drop quality instead of buffering
Metadata & views: view counts via an async aggregation pipeline, not synchronous DB writes

cards.title: Design a Distributed Rate Limiter
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for limiting request rates across a fleet.

functional: Allow/deny a request based on a per-client limit (e.g. 100 req/min)
Work consistently across many app servers
nonfunctional: Very low latency (it's in the hot path of every request)
Highly available; approximate limits acceptable under failure
Scales with request volume
hld: Rate-limit check runs at the API gateway / middleware
Shared counter store (Redis) keyed by clientId so all servers agree
On each request: atomically increment & compare to limit
deepDives: Algorithm: token bucket (allows bursts, smooth refill) or sliding-window counter (accurate, avoids fixed-window edge bursts)
Atomicity across servers: Redis INCR/EXPIRE or a Lua script for check-and-increment in one round trip
Failure mode: fail-open (allow) vs fail-closed; local in-memory fallback + async sync to reduce Redis pressure

cards.title: Design a Web Crawler
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for crawling the web at scale.

functional: Fetch pages starting from seed URLs
Extract links and keep crawling
Store page content for downstream (e.g. indexing)
nonfunctional: Massive scale (billions of pages); high throughput
Politeness — don't hammer one domain; respect robots.txt
Fault tolerant & resumable
hld: URL frontier (queue) -> fetcher workers -> parser -> store + extract links -> back into frontier
Seen-URL set to avoid re-crawling
Content written to blob store
deepDives: Frontier design: prioritized + per-domain queues with rate limits for politeness
Dedup: bloom filter / hashed URL set for 'have I seen this?' at scale; content hashing for near-duplicate pages
Scale & resilience: partition the frontier by domain hash; workers are stateless & retry; checkpoint progress

functional: Send notifications across channels (push, SMS, email)
Support user preferences and templates
nonfunctional: High throughput, spiky load; at-least-once delivery
Decoupled from senders; resilient to third-party (APNs/FCM/Twilio) failures
hld: Producers publish notification events to a message queue (Kafka)
Channel workers consume, render templates, call the provider (APNs/FCM/SES/Twilio)
Preference service filters/opt-outs before dispatch
deepDives: Queue decouples spikes from delivery; per-channel worker pools scale independently
Reliability: retries with backoff + dead-letter queue; idempotency keys to avoid duplicate sends
Rate limiting per provider; batching; user-level dedup/throttle so users aren't spammed

cards.title: Design a Leaderboard / Top-K
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for a real-time ranked leaderboard.

functional: Update a player's score
Get top-K players and a player's own rank
nonfunctional: Low-latency reads of ranks; frequent score updates
Real-time-ish; huge number of players
hld: Redis Sorted Set (ZSET) per leaderboard: score-ordered, O(log n) update, O(log n + k) top-k
Writes update the ZSET; reads query ranks directly
Durable copy of scores in a DB behind the cache
deepDives: Sorted set gives rank & top-k cheaply; ZADD to update, ZREVRANGE for top-k, ZREVRANK for a player's rank
Scale: shard leaderboards by game/region; for one giant board, partition by score range and merge top-k across shards
Time windows (daily/weekly): separate ZSETs with TTL; persist to DB for history

cards.title: Design an Ad Click Aggregator
cards.front: Walk R-C-A-H-D for aggregating ad-click metrics.

functional: Ingest ad-click events at high volume
Query aggregated metrics (clicks per ad over time windows)
nonfunctional: Very high write throughput; near-real-time analytics
Durability of raw events; approximate is OK for fast dashboards
This is a data-pipeline question — add a Data Flow
hld: Clicks -> ingestion service -> stream (Kafka)
Stream processor (Flink/Spark) aggregates into time buckets
Aggregates written to an OLAP store; queried by dashboards
deepDives: Two paths (lambda-ish): fast approximate stream aggregation for live dashboards + a batch job over raw events for exact/corrected numbers
Windowing: tumbling windows (e.g. per-minute) keyed by adId; handle late events with a grace period
Scale writes: partition the stream by adId; keep raw events in cheap durable storage for reprocessing
